danger_signals.visual_form: Environmental hazards within each sport event (surfboard wipeouts, BMX crashes, footbag misses)
danger_signals.meaning: Physical failure states; loss of performance score

danger_signals.visual_form: Slowing animation in roller skating and BMX events
danger_signals.meaning: Worsening performance state; risk of course timeout

danger_signals.visual_form: Character sprite tumbling or stopping abruptly

safety_signals.visual_form: Open skateable/rideable terrain with visible boundaries
Flat, unobstructed visual surface in skating/BMX sections
Visible incoming surf with clear rolling water animation
safety_signals.meaning: Surfable opportunity; safe zone for the active event

faction_indicators.symbol_or_sigil: Half-pipe symbol, surfboard icon, BMX bike icon, footbag icon, roller skates icon, flying disc icon
faction_indicators.environmental_markers: California-themed locations: beach (surfing), skate parks (half-pipe, BMX), urban areas (roller skating), open fields (footbag, flying disc)

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Active sport scene with animated character, moving obstacles, scrolling/parallax background
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Player character continuously moving/performing; real-time score counter active; timer running

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Final score screen with player results, rankings comparison, hi-score table display
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Input acceptance for menu navigation; score locked; transition to next event or menu state

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Smooth animation, character maintaining stable position, positive visual feedback (score increments)
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Continued forward momentum; score accumulation; no collision states triggered

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Character animation showing strain or disruption; visual shake or stagger effect
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Reduced movement speed; score stagnation or penalty; collision/fall animation triggers

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Event selection screen with 6 sport icons selectable; visual menu layout with California thematic elements
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Input acceptance for sport selection; transition between different event engines

environmental_storytelling.example: Surfing event set on California beach with ocean background; half-pipe in concrete skate park with urban architecture; footbag in open outdoor plaza
Player name entry field allows custom identification; specific names (PAUL/RACHEL) trigger hidden developer message (PP & RME) on rollerskating course ground texture
Hi-score table display with named entries; developer names (Paul Proctor, Rachel M. Edwards initials) embedded in default records communicates authorship and game persistence
European version removed developer name references from hi-score table and rollerskating ground text; surfing theme received new musical composition by David Wise, signaling content localization

entity_behavioral_tells.behavior: Continuous animation cycle with directional response to input; physics-based movement (momentum, gravity in surfing/BMX)
Immediate animation stop or fall frame; loss of forward momentum; temporary control lockout
Static or predictable movement patterns; visual contrast to safe terrain; collision detection triggers penalty
Animated rolling motion; player must be positioned to ride it; missed waves trigger failure state
Real-time increment on successful actions; visual prominence on screen; updates with each positive action execution

notes: California Games NES (1989, Rare) is a non-narrative sports collection game with minimal world-state complexity. Danger and safety signals are purely mechanical/performance-based rather than thematic. No faction warfare or political systems exist. Environmental storytelling relies primarily on location authenticity (California-specific sports venues) and subtle developer signatures. The game's primary manifestation system communicates player performance state through animation, collision, and score feedback rather than world narrative. Regional differences (music swap, text removal) indicate post-development content adaptation but no gameplay world-state changes. The paradigm is purely competitive sports mechanics with no emergent narrative or political simulation elements.
sources_used: tcrf - The Cutting Room Floor (California Games NES article)
